An experiment done with chocolate chip cookies illustrates the principle of scarcity very well. In this study, participants were asked to rate cookies on their desirability, their attractiveness, and how much people would be willing to pay for them. As with most studies like this, participants were randomly assigned to different groups. The first group was asked to rate the treats presented to them in a jar holding ten cookies. The second group was asked to rate the same cookies in a jar which contained only two cookies. The jars for each group were identical, as were the cookies in the jars. The only difference was that one jar had ten cookies and the other jar had only two. Despite this, the participants who got only two cookies rated them as more desirable, more attractive, and worth paying more for relative to the participants who had gotten ten. Because the people in the two cookie conditions saw the treats as scarce, they wanted them more and found them more desirable. But there's an interesting question. Do you think that they also rated the scarcer cookies as tastier than the more plentiful ones? Actually no. They didn't find the scarce cookies tastier even though they were rated as more desirable. This is because scarcity doesn't change the objective features of a thing. It just changes people's desire for those features. In the case of the cookies, scarcity didn't change the cookies' sensory aspects, so the cookies tasted exactly the same. People just wanted them more when they were scarce, just like they want almost anything more when it is scarce.
